spec.question: How many home runs did Derek Jeter hit in May 1998?
spec.notes: Phase 1 Q1. 4 HRs: 5/2 vs KCA, 5/6 vs TEX, 5/13 vs TEX, 5/15 vs MIN.

questions.response_text: Derek Jeter hit **4 home runs** in May 1998. That was part of his strong 1998 season, which was one of the best of his career as the Yankees went on to win 114 regular-season games and the World Series that year.

tool_input.query:
SELECT SUM(hr) AS home_runs
FROM retro.plays p
JOIN retro.games g ON p.gid = g.gid
WHERE p.batter = 'jeted001'
  AND g.gametype = 'regular'
  AND EXTRACT(YEAR FROM g.date) = 1998
  AND EXTRACT(MONTH FROM g.date) = 5

spec.question: How many home runs did Mark McGwire hit in 1998?
spec.notes: Phase 1 Q2. Record-breaking season; #62 off Trachsel 9/8, #70 off Pavano 9/27.

questions.response_text:
**70.** Mark McGwire hit 70 home runs in the 1998 regular season, breaking Roger Maris's single-season record of 61 that had stood since 1961. It was one of the most celebrated chases in baseball history, with McGwire and Sammy Sosa trading the lead back and forth all summer.

Want me to break it down further — by month, home vs. away, or against specific opponents?

tool_input.query:
SELECT SUM(hr) AS home_runs
FROM retro.plays p
JOIN retro.games g ON p.gid = g.gid
WHERE p.batter = 'mcgwm001'
  AND EXTRACT(YEAR FROM g.date) = 1998
  AND g.gametype = 'regular'

spec.question: Who were the best left-handed batters against left-handed pitchers in 1998?
spec.notes: Phase 1 Q3. Top with 150-AB floor: Olerud .375, Vaughn .340, Greer .328, Floyd .325, Vina .324. Good answer surfaces the AB threshold (Andy Fox / dynamism principle, CLAUDE.md §8). Olerud must appear; threshold-surfacing and switch-hitter handling judged on manual review.

questions.response_text:
Here's the 1998 lefty-vs-lefty leaderboard (min. 50 PA), ranked by OPS:

| Rank | Player | PA | AVG | OBP | SLG | OPS | HR |
|------|--------|----|-----|-----|-----|-----|----|
| 1 | **Ken Griffey Jr.** | 197 | .299 | .360 | .701 | 1.061 | 21 |
| 2 | **Mo Vaughn** | 220 | .333 | .400 | .586 | .986 | 14 |
| 3 | **John Olerud** | 182 | .375 | .467 | .513 | .980 | 5 |
| 4 | **Rafael Palmeiro** | 256 | .317 | .387 | .591 | .978 | 15 |
| 5 | **Larry Walker** | 153 | .321 | .412 | .511 | .923 | 3 |
| 6 | **Barry Bonds** | 212 | .281 | .420 | .497 | .917 | 9 |
| 7 | **Will Clark** | 166 | .327 | .386 | .517 | .903 | 5 |
| 8 | **Matt Lawton** | 158 | .275 | .392 | .504 | .896 | 6 |
| 9 | **Tony Gwynn** | 173 | .323 | .358 | .534 | .892 | 8 |
| 10 | **Jim Thome** | 166 | .289 | .386 | .500 | .886 | 7 |

### Highlights

- **Griffey** sits alone at the top with a monster .701 SLG against lefties — 21 home runs in just 197 PA is absurd, one every 9.4 PA. He completely obliterated the platoon disadvantage.
- **Olerud** is the contact/OBP standout: .375/.467 with almost no strikeouts (22 in 182 PA) — a nightmare to pitch to even if you had the "platoon edge."
- **Bonds** takes the patient approach — 39 walks in 212 PA (.420 OBP) while keeping his average alive at .281.
- **Palmeiro** had the most PA of anyone in the top 5 (256), meaning opposing managers weren't shying away from lefties even against him — and he punished them anyway.
- **Tony Gwynn** at age 38 still hit .323 against lefties with only 6 strikeouts in 173 PA — vintage Gwynn contact wizardry.

The conventional wisdom about the lefty-lefty platoon split clearly didn't apply to most of these guys in '98. Want me to flip it and look at which lefty batters were most *vulnerable* to lefty pitchers that year, or dig into a specific player's breakdown?

tool_input.query:
SELECT
    p.batter,
    pl.usename,
    pl.lastname,
    SUM(p.pa) AS pa,
    SUM(p.ab) AS ab,
    SUM(p.single + p.double + p.triple + p.hr) AS hits,
    SUM(p.hr) AS hr,
    SUM(p.walk) AS bb,
    SUM(p.k) AS k,
    SUM(p.double) AS doubles,
    SUM(p.triple) AS triples,
    ROUND(SUM(p.single + p.double + p.triple + p.hr)::numeric / NULLIF(SUM(p.ab), 0), 3) AS avg,
    ROUND((SUM(p.single + p.double + p.triple + p.hr) + SUM(p.walk) + SUM(p.hbp))::numeric / NULLIF(SUM(p.pa), 0), 3) AS obp,
    ROUND((SUM(p.single) + 2*SUM(p.double) + 3*SUM(p.triple) + 4*SUM(p.hr))::numeric / NULLIF(SUM(p.ab), 0), 3) AS slg
FROM retro.plays p
JOIN retro.games g ON p.gid = g.gid
JOIN retro.players pl ON p.batter = pl.id
WHERE g.gametype = 'regular'
  AND EXTRACT(YEAR FROM g.date) = 1998
  AND p.bathand = 'L'
  AND p.pithand = 'L'
GROUP BY p.batter, pl.usename, pl.lastname
HAVING SUM(p.pa) >= 50
ORDER BY (SUM(p.single + p.double + p.triple + p.hr) + SUM(p.walk) + SUM(p.hbp))::numeric / NULLIF(SUM(p.pa), 0)
       + (SUM(p.single) + 2*SUM(p.double) + 3*SUM(p.triple) + 4*SUM(p.hr))::numeric / NULLIF(SUM(p.ab), 0) DESC
LIMIT 20;

spec.question: Who hit for the cycle in 1998?
spec.notes: Phase 1 Q4. Three cycles: Mike Blowers (5/18 at CHA), Dante Bichette (6/10 vs TEX), Neifi Perez (7/25 vs SLN). All three names should appear; any extra names are likely false positives worth investigating.

questions.response_text:
Three players hit for the cycle in 1998:

| Date | Player | Batting Team | Opponent |
|------|--------|-------------|----------|
| May 18 | **Mike Blowers** | Oakland | at Chicago (AL) |
| June 10 | **Dante Bichette** | Colorado | vs. Texas |
| July 25 | **Neifi Perez** | Colorado | vs. St. Louis |

Notable that two of the three happened at Coors Field — not a total surprise given the offensive environment there in the late '90s. Bichette and Perez were both Rockies, and Colorado was a cycle machine in that era. Blowers got his with Oakland at Comiskey.

Want me to dig into the details of any of these games — the full box score, how many RBI they drove in, etc.?

tool_input.query:
SELECT 
    p.batter,
    pl.usename,
    pl.lastname,
    p.gid,
    g.date,
    p.batteam,
    p.pitteam,
    SUM(p.single) AS singles,
    SUM(p.double) AS doubles,
    SUM(p.triple) AS triples,
    SUM(p.hr) AS hrs
FROM retro.plays p
JOIN retro.games g ON p.gid = g.gid
JOIN retro.players pl ON p.batter = pl.id
WHERE EXTRACT(YEAR FROM g.date) = 1998
  AND g.gametype = 'regular'
GROUP BY p.batter, pl.usename, pl.lastname, p.gid, g.date, p.batteam, p.pitteam
HAVING SUM(p.single) >= 1
   AND SUM(p.double) >= 1
   AND SUM(p.triple) >= 1
   AND SUM(p.hr) >= 1
ORDER BY g.date;

spec.question: What was the 1998 Yankees' regular-season record?
spec.notes: Phase 1 Q5. 162 games, 114-48, .704. Substrings allow varied phrasings ('114 wins, 48 losses', '114-48').

questions.response_text: The 1998 Yankees went **114-48** in the regular season — one of the greatest records in baseball history. That .704 winning percentage held up as the best in the American League for decades. They then went on to win the World Series that year, finishing the postseason 11-2 for a combined 125 wins on the season.

tool_input.query:
SELECT
  SUM(CASE WHEN wteam = 'NYA' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS wins,
  SUM(CASE WHEN lteam = 'NYA' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS losses
FROM retro.games
WHERE (hometeam = 'NYA' OR visteam = 'NYA')
  AND gametype = 'regular'
  AND EXTRACT(YEAR FROM date) = 1998

spec.question: How many career home runs did Babe Ruth hit?

questions.response_text:
**714 regular-season home runs** — the canonical number that stood as the all-time record until Hank Aaron broke it in 1974.

The full play-by-play log (including postseason) shows **730 total**, meaning Ruth hit 16 home runs in World Series play, which checks out across his 10 Fall Classic appearances.

His career spanned 1914–1935, primarily with the Yankees, and the 714 figure is the one enshrined in baseball history.

tool_input.query:
SELECT SUM(hr) AS career_hr
FROM retro.plays
WHERE batter = 'ruthb101'

SELECT SUM(hr) AS regular_season_hr
FROM retro.plays p
JOIN retro.games g ON p.gid = g.gid
WHERE p.batter = 'ruthb101'
  AND g.gametype = 'regular'

spec.question: How many career home runs did Hank Aaron hit?

questions.response_text: Hank Aaron hit **755 regular-season home runs** over his career (1954–1976) — the all-time record until Barry Bonds surpassed it in 2007. The slightly higher total of 763 includes postseason play.

tool_input.query:
SELECT SUM(hr) AS career_hr
FROM retro.plays
WHERE batter = 'aaroh101'

SELECT SUM(hr) AS career_hr
FROM retro.plays
WHERE batter = 'aaroh101'
  AND gid IN (SELECT gid FROM retro.games WHERE gametype = 'regular')

spec.question: How many home runs did Barry Bonds hit in 2001?

questions.response_text: **73.** Barry Bonds hit 73 home runs in the 2001 regular season, setting the all-time single-season record that still stands today.

tool_input.query:
SELECT SUM(hr) AS home_runs
FROM retro.plays p
JOIN retro.games g ON p.gid = g.gid
WHERE p.batter = 'bondb001'
  AND EXTRACT(YEAR FROM g.date) = 2001
  AND g.gametype = 'regular'

spec.question: How many hits did Ichiro Suzuki get in 2004?
spec.notes: Phase 2 Layer 2 verified: single-season hits record, 262.

questions.response_text: **262 hits** — Ichiro's legendary 2004 season, which set the all-time single-season record for hits, surpassing George Sisler's 257 from 1920. That's still the record today.

tool_input.query:
SELECT SUM(b_h) AS hits
FROM retro.batting
WHERE id = 'suzui001'
  AND stattype = 'value'
  AND gid IN (
    SELECT gid FROM retro.games
    WHERE EXTRACT(YEAR FROM date) = 2004
      AND gametype = 'regular'
  )

spec.question: What was the most lopsided trade in baseball history measured by WAR?
spec.notes: Retrosheet does not include WAR or trade records (CLAUDE.md §7). Correct behavior: acknowledge WAR is out of scope, decline the lopsided-trade-by-WAR comparison, pivot to related stats the system can answer (career batting/pitching value the player produced for each franchise, etc.). Confident WAR figures or external-source citations are hallucinations. must_not_contain catches the most obvious red flags; the decline-signal check (in checks.py) verifies the agent acknowledged the limit; manual review judges pivot quality.

questions.response_text:
WAR isn't part of the Retrosheet database — it's a derived metric (Wins Above Replacement) that isn't stored here. I can't answer the WAR-based version of this question.

What I *can* do is look at specific trades from a counting/rate-stats angle. For example, if you name a trade you're curious about — say, the Nolan Ryan–for–Jim Fregosi deal, or the Frank Robinson trade, or any other — I can pull career stats for both sides of the deal, covering the years *after* the trade was made, and give you a clear picture of who outproduced whom. Counting stats (HR, RBI, hits, ERA, strikeouts) and rate stats (AVG, OBP, SLG, ERA+) won't give you a single WAR number, but they can absolutely tell a compelling story about lopsidedness.

Want to pick a trade and dig in?
